title: Lesson 392
transcript: I just realized that Rob always wears his nice destination certification sweatshirts and so on. And I should be too. So here I am. Nice theme. We're going to get you to the top of the CISSP mountain. That's the whole idea here. All right. Let's get into this next topic here. Earlier, when we talked about topologies, We talked about bus topology and star and mesh and ring and tree and so on. A lot of those topologies have this problem of collisions. So the industry has to obviously address collisions. That's what this section is going to focus on right so if you imagine that bus topology for example where every device is connected to a single bus which is essentially a wire if two devices send information at the same time gonna result in a collision, right? So we have to be able to handle collisions. And the industry has really come up with really three ways to handle collisions and be able to deal with them efficiently. and the two that you see on the outside here polling and token based we already talked about token based and i think i said that an example of that would have been ibm token ring where you have this token that's being passed from device to device, right? So the idea is if you need to send information, you have to wait for that token to get to you. And that could be problematic, first of all, if it's a large network, because then you have to wait, it's not efficient. Secondly, the biggest problem with that, too, is that if a device goes down, then maybe it may impact the entire network because that token cannot pass from one device to the next if there's one in between that has gone down, for example. It's basically why token-based handling of collisions has really gone away. And those networks have really disappeared as well. Polling used to be popular in the past as well. You may still see it, and that's why we cover it here. But in the old days, ArcNet type of networks would have used this. And basically what this means is that if you're part of a network, every device is asking or polling every other device whether that device has any information for you. All right. So the idea is, you know, I'm asking everybody that's part of my segment or network saying, OK, do you have any information for me? Okay, no, I'll move on to the next one. Do you have any information for me? Do you have any information for me? And so this isn't very efficient because while I'm doing this with every device that I'm connected to, so is everyone else. So there's a lot of energy, for example, that is being wasted by devices asking others whether there's any information. so that's gone away as well everything today is basically what you see here this carrier sense multiple access csma for short and if you think about these words right carrier sense multiple access tells you exactly how this works right the idea is and again if you picture bus topology In your mind, all devices are connected to that bus, which is essentially the carrier, right? And because you're connected to the bus, the wire, you can sense it. So the idea is, if you've got any information to send, you have to wait for that wire to become free. And so the idea is you wait and you wait until it becomes free. And how will you know when it does become free? Well, typically the way we send bits across media is using voltage, electricity. So the idea is if you've got information to send, You've got to wait for that wire to have zero voltage on it. In fact, I think what we use is plus five volts to represent a one and a zero is minus five volts. So if you're waiting to send information, guess what? You have to wait for that wire to have zero voltage across and then you send your information. And then you wait for an acknowledgement basically to come back from the machine that you sent it to. That's basically what this really indicates, right? You just have to wait for the wire to become free and then you send information. that's not going to totally eliminate the opportunity of collisions because collisions are still going to happen. Here's a scenario where that might happen. Let's say there's two devices. that are waiting to send information right so we're both sensing the wire and as soon as the wire becomes free guess what we're both going to detect that at the same time and probably send information at the same time which is going to result in a collision. So collisions are still going to happen even though we wait for the wire to become free. And that's why there are two flavors of CSMA. There's CSMA with collision avoidance, which means we totally avoid collisions. And the way that this is handled is that instead of, for example, using one wire to connect devices together, we use two. And so one wire is used to receive information. The other one is used to send information. So the idea is you've got lanes of traffic. And if you've got lanes of traffic, collisions should never happen. And this conceptually is the concept behind this. Instead of lanes or specific physical wires, we can use different frequencies and so on to receive and send information to avoid collisions. And very appropriate symbol that we've used on top of this because a technology that actually uses CSMA with collision avoidance are all of your wireless standards like 802.11 b g n a c a x and so on. They all use CSMA with collision avoidance. And so when you're talking to a wireless access point or your wireless router, for example, we're sending and receiving bits, but we're using different lanes to do that so that collisions are actually avoided. All wired LANs and technologies and networks will use CSMA with collision detection. So collisions will happen, but you have the ability to be able to detect them. And so the idea is, again, we're waiting for the wire to become free. When it does, we're going to send our information. But because you're still sensing the wire, you'll be able to detect collisions if they happen, right? Because a collision will cause the voltage to spike. So if two bits collide, for example, that's going to cause the voltage to spike. And so if you've just sent information, you're still monitoring the wire. And if you detect a collision that just happened, a spike in the voltage, then you can say, oh, I just sent information. that probably has affected my transmission. I need to resend the information, right? So that's what CSMA with collision detection tells you exactly what these do, right? We avoid collisions here. Wireless is what uses that. Collision detection, CSMA with collision detection is what all wired LANs and networks will use where we have the ability to detect collisions if they happen and resend the information.
